request.question: Cult of Personality is a song used by CM Punk as an entrance song for the WWE event held at which New Jersey stadium?

title: WrestleMania 29
Champion (Clement Marfo & The Frontline song)
Luke Gallows
WWE Slam City
King of the Ring (2010)
Elimination Chamber (2013)
Royal Rumble (2013)
Judgment of Princeton
Cult of Personality (song)
WrestleMania XXX
sentences: WrestleMania 29 (billed in print ads as WrestleMania NY/NJ) was the twenty-ninth annual WrestleMania professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) event produced by WWE, held on April 7, 2013 at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey.
Nine professional wrestling matches were contested at the event, with one being shown on the pre-show.
"Champion" is a single release by English group Clement Marfo & The Frontline.
The song was released in the United Kingdom as a digital download on July 20, 2012.
The song peaked to number 38 on the UK Singles Chart.
The song features on the soundtrack of the video game "F1 2011" and the 2010 film "StreetDance 3D", and was the official theme song of the WWE Royal Rumble pay-per-view in 2013.
The song was also used CM Punk's 434 day reign as WWE Champion as a tribute
Andrew William Hankinson (born December 22, 1983) is an American professional wrestler and promoter, currently signed with WWE on the Raw brand under the ring name Luke Gallows.

During his first stint with the promotion, Hankinson also appeared briefly as an imposter of the wrestler Kane during 2006 and then returned as Festus, a seemingly mentally handicapped character who teamed with Jesse (Ray Gordy), but the team achieved little success.

After disappearing from television, Hankinson later became the enforcer and "disciple" of CM Punk, sporting a cleaner, militant look, with the Festus character's "real identity" revealed as Luke Gallows.
His first run in WWE ended in 2010.

He is perhaps best known for working for New Japan Pro Wrestling under the ring name Doc Gallows, and he also wrestled prominently under the TNA banner, where he was a member of the heel Aces & Eights stable under the ring name D.O.C. (Director of Chaos).

Along with Karl Anderson, Hankinson has won the WWE Raw Tag Team Championship once since returning to WWE, and he also held the IWGP Tag Team Championship three times with Anderson, making them one of only three tag teams (with The Steiner Brothers and The Dudley Boyz) to have held both titles between those promotions.
WWE Slam City was an animated show produced by WWE, which aired on the WWE Network and Wednesday evenings on Nicktoons.
WWE Slam City was based on the Mattel action figure series starring toyetic versions of the WWE roster circa 2013.

The series, filmed in the next generation of stop-motion animation, features a new WWE animated character The Finisher, who fires all of the WWE wrestlers and sends them to Slam City to find day jobs.
The wrestlers are plunged into new career challenges as they pack every street corner with work to do and scores to settle.

With John Cena as an auto mechanic, Rey Mysterio as a traffic guard, Alberto Del Rio as a coffee house barista, Mark Henry as a pizza shop mascot, CM Punk as an ice-cream man, Dolph Ziggler as an industry leader, Randy Orton as a zookeeper, Kane as a chef and Sheamus as a theater usher.
Daniel Bryan, The Miz, Big Show, Brock Lesnar, Santino Marella and Damien Sandow are still jobless but Santino and Sandow are the most desperate to find one.
WWE Legends Stone Cold and The Rock are also featured in Slam City.
The 'Stretch Move' feature of Slam City characters is highlighted in WWE Slam City.
The 2010 King of the Ring was a special edition of "Raw".
that aired on November 29, 2010, at the Wells Fargo Center in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.
The program featured the nineteenth King of the Ring tournament and first since 2008.
Sheamus defeated John Morrison in the finals of the tournament to become King of the Ring.

Unlike the previous tournament that featured wrestlers from the "Raw," "SmackDown" and "ECW" brands, the 2010 version of the tournament only featured wrestlers from "Raw" and "SmackDown" (following the closure of the ECW brand in February 2010).

The last King of the Ring tournament which only featured wrestlers from both "Raw" and "SmackDown" was held in 2002 (following the WWE Brand Extension the previous month, but before the ECW brand was introduced as a third brand in 2006).

Despite the event being mainly focused on the King of the Ring concept, the main event featured Jerry Lawler (who was celebrating his birthday that day) facing the new WWE Champion The Miz for the WWE Championship in a Tables, Ladders and Chairs match which marked Lawler's first WWE Championship match.
Lawler lost due to distractions from Alex Riley and Michael Cole.
CM Punk was joined in commentary on the show.
Elimination Chamber (2013) (also known as No Escape (2013) in Germany) was a professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) event produced by WWE.
It took place on February 17, 2013, at the New Orleans Arena in New Orleans, Louisiana.
The pay-per-view was the fourth annual Elimination Chamber event.
This year's event was headlined by The Rock defending the WWE Championship against CM Punk.
Royal Rumble (2013) was the twenty-sixth annual Royal Rumble professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) event produced by WWE.
It took place on January 27, 2013 at the US Airways Center in Phoenix, Arizona.
It was the first WWE pay-per-view of 2013, and featured John Cena winning the traditional Royal Rumble match.
In the main event, The Rock defeated CM Punk for the WWE Championship, recapturing the title for the first time since 2002, and ending Punk's long reign at 434 days.

The Judgment of Princeton was a wine tasting (or blind tasting) event held on 8 June 2012 during a conference of the American Association of Wine Economists held at Princeton University in Princeton, New Jersey.

The purpose of this event was to compare, by a blind tasting, of several French wines against wines produced in New Jersey in order to gauge the quality and development of the New Jersey wine industry.
Because New Jersey's wine industry is relatively young and small, it has received little attention in the world wine market.

The state's wine production has experienced growth in recent years largely as a result of state legislators offering new opportunities for winery licensing and repealing Prohibition-era laws that have constrained the industry's development in past years.

This event was modeled after a 1976 blind tasting event dubbed the "Judgment of Paris" in which French wines were compared to several wines produced in California when that state's wine industry was similarly young and developing.
The New Jersey wine industry heralded the results and asserted that the rating of New Jersey wines by the blind tasting's judges was a victory for the state's wine industry.
"Cult of Personality" is a song by rock band Living Colour.
It was their second single off their debut album, "Vivid", released on July 14, 1988.
"Cult of Personality" reached No. 13 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and No. 9 on the "Billboard" Album Rock Tracks chart.
It also won the Grammy Award for Best Hard Rock Performance in 1990.
Its music video earned the MTV Video Music Award for Best Group Video and MTV Video Music Award for Best New Artist.
The song was ranked No. 69 on VH1's "100 Greatest Hard Rock Songs".
The solo was ranked No. 87 in "Guitar World"' s "100 Greatest Guitar Solos" list.
It was also selected for inclusion in the musical reference book, "1001 Songs You Must Hear Before You Die: And 10,001 You Must Download".
The sections before the bridge are similar to a hook in Black Sabbath's "Wheels Of Confusion".
In 2007, the song was re-recorded and released for the video game "".
The re-recording later appeared in "Guitar Hero Smash Hits".
It also appeared in the video game "" on the radio station "Radio X".
The song is also the entrance music for former ROH and WWE wrestler turned UFC fighter, CM Punk, and Living Colour performed the song live for his entrance at WrestleMania 29.
The song also appeared on the soundtrack for the 2015 video game "NBA 2K16".
WrestleMania XXX (also written as WrestleMania 30) was the thirtieth annual WrestleMania professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) event produced by WWE.
It took place on April 6, 2014, at the Mercedes-Benz Superdome in New Orleans, Louisiana.
It was the first WWE event simultaneously broadcast live on pay-per-view television and WWE's new streaming media service, the WWE Network.
Seven professional wrestling matches were scheduled on the event's card and one pre-show match was streamed on the WWE Network.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

The `summary` must be a concise (typically 1–4 short sentences or clauses), self-contained intermediate state that helps the next agent locate the missing second-hop evidence. Do not try to produce the final answer. Follow these rules when composing the `summary`:

1) First-hop evidence: In one short clause, paraphrase or quote (<=15 words) the specific fact from the passages that is relevant to the question and that you are using as the bridge. Include the passage title or identifier that contains this evidence.

2) Bridge / missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity or the specific missing fact that must be resolved in the next hop (for example: an ambiguous given name that must be identified, the named person whose birthplace is required, the tournament title whose venues must be counted, or which film credit remains unverified). If the passages already give a full name, use that full name as the bridge entity; if only a given name or ambiguous identifier is present, state that the identity is unresolved.

3) Retrieval direction: Provide one concise, actionable next-hop retrieval plan (1–3 ordered steps or 1–2 short search-query templates). Use clear placeholders where appropriate (e.g., "fetch the biography page for <NAME>", "search \"<NAME> birthplace\" site:wikipedia.org", "open the '<TOURNAMENT>' page and inspect its Venues/Matches/Schedule section", or "fetch '<FILM>' cast list and compare to '<OTHER FILM>' cast"). Specify which page/title or which page sections the next agent should inspect. Do not perform retrieval or assert any facts that are not present in the given passages.

4) Disambiguation: If the question's referent is ambiguous (e.g., only a given name is provided), request the user's clarification in one short sentence only (e.g., "If you mean a different Maurice, please provide the full name."). Do not ask permission to search; just state the needed clarification.

5) Constraints and tone: Never invent or assert unverified facts (no guessed winners, years, birthplaces, counts, or cast overlaps). Do not output the final answer. Keep language neutral, concise, and actionable. Avoid long verbatim extracts beyond a short supporting quote.

Output exactly one field: `summary` (a single string following the above structure).
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `summary_1`, produce the field `query`.

Output requirements
- Return exactly one compact, single-line retrieval query string in the field `query`. Do not return any other fields, commentary, punctuation, quotes, or the answer.
- Keep the query short and retrieval-oriented (keyword bag, typically ~3–7 meaningful tokens). Prefer tokens already present in `question` or `summary_1`.

Rules for composing the query
1) Nonprofit medical-practice/facility questions: If the question asks about a non-profit/nonprofit medical practice or facility (or uses similar wording) and `summary_1` contains an organization name, do NOT repeat that organization name. Instead produce a concise bridge query combining the place token from the question, the state/region token, and a class term such as "nonprofit medical practice" or "clinic". Use the template: <place> <state> nonprofit medical practice clinic

2) Avoid hypothesizing names: If the needed facility or organization name is not supported by `question` or `summary_1`, do not invent or assert candidate names. Reuse the topic and the expressed information need (topic + class + location) to form the query.

3) Comparative person-role questions: If the question compares two people (e.g., which person fills a larger variety of roles) and the inputs name both persons or `summary_1` lists one person's roles, produce one compact query that names both people and includes concise role/occupation keywords (examples: director producer composer musician writer actor). You may include a brief source token such as Wikipedia to guide retrieval, but remain compact. Do not ask for the final comparison or return conclusions.

General prohibitions
- Do not output full sentences, long explanatory text, lists of candidate organizations, or claims/answers.
- Do not add metadata, instructions, or extra fields—only the single query string.

Produce the `query` field now according to the rules above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, and `passages`, produce the field `summary` (string). Preserve the native input/output interface exactly and return only the `summary` field. Do not produce any other fields, headers, or meta-commentary.

Task: Integrate the newly retrieved `passages` with the existing `context` so the result is a grounded, post-retrieval state that the final-answer agent can use. Keep the summary concise, factual, and limited to information present in `question`, `context`, and `passages`.

Required behavior (apply the relevant rules to each input):
- Preserve facts already asserted in `context` unless the `passages` contradict them. If passages contradict a contextual claim, state the contradiction briefly and retain uncertainty rather than inventing a resolution.
- When a passage (or passages) explicitly supports a claim found in `context`, do all of the following in the `summary`:
  1) Briefly restate the claim from `context` in one short clause.
  2) Identify which retrieved passage(s) provide explicit support by naming the passage title (or a short identifier) in parentheses and give a one-line paraphrase or at most one short quoted fragment drawn directly from that passage as corroboration. Keep paraphrases short and avoid long verbatim spans.
  3) If other passage(s) do not state the claim but supply useful metadata (for example: title, year, director, occupation, full name/alias, location details), list those retained metadata facts in one short clause per passage.
  4) Finish with one concise integration sentence that synthesizes the evidence: state which passage explicitly supports the claim and which passage(s) only supply metadata for downstream use. Resolve the earlier bridge (i.e., commit to the claim) only if explicit support is present; otherwise keep the summary phrased to reflect remaining uncertainty.
- If a passage links two entities (e.g., a facility name to an address or locality) and that link resolves a location or relationship asserted in `context`, include a single short paraphrase that records that linking fact and then a brief integrated sentence that ties that link back to the studio/person/film in question.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Output must be concise (about 1–4 sentences), factual, and limited to content present in the inputs. Prefer 1–3 sentences when the integration is simple.
- Do not add new facts, speculation, or external knowledge beyond `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
- Avoid long quotations; at most one short quoted fragment per supporting passage and only when it directly helps attribute support.
- Do not use hedging language when the passages state facts plainly. Use hedging only when the inputs are ambiguous or contradictory.
- Do not include dataset labels, internal notes, evaluation, or extraneous formatting. Do not produce the final answer outside this `summary` field.

Goal: deliver a compact, evidence-attributed summary that (a) restates the first-hop claim when present, (b) explicitly attributes which passage(s) support that claim and which provide metadata, and (c) synthesizes the evidence so the downstream final-answer agent can use it to answer the question.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional conservative, evidence-driven normalizations and tie-breaking rules (small, shared improvements):
- Use summary_2 as the later integrated evidence and give it priority when summaries disagree about the answer, but do not ignore an explicit short named-entity asserted clearly in summary_1: if either summary contains a concise explicit attribution (for example, phrasing like "directed by <NAME>", "headquartered in <PLACE>", or a direct statement that names the immediate jurisdiction), accept that explicit short named-entity as the supported minimal answer unless the other summary gives a different explicit short named-entity (in that conflicting-explicit case, emit a brief clarification question). In short: prefer explicit concise assertions wherever present; otherwise prefer summary_2 when resolving conflicts.
- When the two summaries agree exactly on a short named phrase that includes a trailing parenthetical disambiguator or trailing parenthetical abbreviation (final ' ( ... )'), strip that single final parenthetical and emit the pre-parenthesis core name (trim surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. Similarly, when the identical agreed phrase ends with the exact suffix ", United States", remove that trailing ", United States" and emit the remaining shorter place string.
- More generally, when an agreed short answer contains trailing clarifying material (a final parentheses block, a trailing parenthetical abbreviation, or an appositive beginning with a comma) and the core name up to the first '(' or the first comma appears verbatim in both summaries, emit that core substring rather than the longer qualified form.
- If the question explicitly requests a size (phrases like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size') and both summaries are identical and contain a phrase of the form '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less', emit only the '<size>' portion (number plus unit) verbatim as the minimal answer (remove the trailing inclusive qualifier).
- If the chosen minimal answer candidate is a contiguous digits-only token sequence (regex ^\d+$), convert it to its spelled-out English words (lowercase, with conventional hyphenation) and emit that word-form as the answer.
- For 'what do X and Y have in common' style questions where both summaries contain the same plural noun token as the shared attribute, emit the singular form of that noun (apply simple regular singularization rules such as removing a trailing 's' or converting 'ies' -> 'y').
- Always prefer a concise explicit short named-entity present verbatim in either summary over asking for clarification when the other summary merely reports inability or uncertainty; however, if the summaries provide two different explicit names for the same field, emit a concise clarification question rather than guessing.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

hop1_docs: Cult of Personality (song) | "Cult of Personality" is a song by rock band Living Colour.  It was their second single off their debut album, "Vivid", released on July 14, 1988.  "Cult of Personality" reached No. 13 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and No. 9 on the "Billboard" Album Rock Tracks chart.  It also won the Grammy Award for Best Hard Rock Performance in 1990.  Its music video earned the MTV Video Music Award for Best Group Video and MTV Video Music Award for Best New Artist.  The song was ranked No. 69 on VH1's "100 Greatest Hard Rock Songs".  The solo was ranked No. 87 in "Guitar World"' s "100 Greatest Guitar Solos" list.  It was also selected for inclusion in the musical reference book, "1001 Songs You Must Hear Before You Die: And 10,001 You Must Download".  The sections before the bridge are similar to a hook in Black Sabbath's "Wheels Of Confusion".  In 2007, the song was re-recorded and released for the video game "".  The re-recording later appeared in "Guitar Hero Smash Hits".  It also appeared in the video game "" on the radio station "Radio X".  The song is also the entrance music for former ROH and WWE wrestler turned UFC fighter, CM Punk, and Living Colour performed the song live for his entrance at WrestleMania 29.  The song also appeared on the soundtrack for the 2015 video game "NBA 2K16".

WWE: Uncaged | WWE: Uncaged is a compilation album of unreleased professional wrestling entrance theme songs which was released by WWE on December 16, 2016 on online music stores.  The album features multiple tracks that were not available to the general public before the release of the album, most notably the first version of Chris Jericho's theme song "Break the Walls Down", which was composed by Jim Johnston and performed by Adam Morenoff used for his WWE debut back in 1999.  The album also features the acoustic versions of Batista's theme song, "I Walk Alone" and Randy Orton's theme song, "Voices" which were used for their WrestleMania 30 video packages, which aired on WWE programming before WrestleMania to get fans hyped and excited for their matches at the event.

Champion (Clement Marfo & The Frontline song) | "Champion" is a single release by English group Clement Marfo & The Frontline.  The song was released in the United Kingdom as a digital download on July 20, 2012.  The song peaked to number 38 on the UK Singles Chart.  The song features on the soundtrack of the video game "F1 2011" and the 2010 film "StreetDance 3D", and was the official theme song of the WWE Royal Rumble pay-per-view in 2013.  The song was also used CM Punk's 434 day reign as WWE Champion as a tribute

WWE No Mercy | No Mercy is an annual professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) event produced by WWE.  The first No Mercy was held on May 16, 1999 in Manchester, England.  The event was held annually in October from 1999 to 2008, and from 2003 to 2006 it was exclusive to the SmackDown!  brand.  In 2016, the event returned as a "SmackDown" exclusive following the reinstated brand extension and aired on the WWE Network for the first time.  However in 2017, the pay-per-view will be a Raw branded event.  The song "No Mercy" by Jim Johnston was used as a regular theme song for the events in 2002, 2004, 2006, and 2007.  In the 2016 and 2017 events, the theme song was "No Mercy" by KIT.

Line in the Sand (Motörhead song) | Line in the Sand is a 2004 rock song from WWE's soundtrack album, "".  The song used to be the entrance theme of the professional wrestling alliance, Evolution.  The song is performed by Motörhead.

WWE SmackDown Road to WrestleMania 22 Tour | The WWE SmackDown Road to WrestleMania 22 Tour was a professional wrestling televised event held by World Wrestling Entertainment and which took place at the Westpac Stadium in Wellington, New Zealand on March 4, 2006.  The event was taped and later aired on WWE 24/7 in the United States.

Stadium anthem | Stadium anthems or sports anthems or arena anthems are a musical genre identifying songs that are played over the public address systems at stadiums and arenas during breaks in the action to rally the fans.  Unlike college fight songs, most stadium anthems were not written primarily for use at sports events, though compilations such as "ESPN Presents Stadium Anthems" and the ""Jock"" series occasionally feature remixed versions of these songs designed to segue together or to accentuate the rhythm or other elements of the songs.  Some football events have their own anthems, but they are not played during breaks, but they are played as entrance music.  The most notable anthems are the FIFA Anthem and the UEFA Champions League Anthem.
hop1_titles: WWE SmackDown Road to WrestleMania 22 Tour
response.summary_1: First-hop evidence: "Living Colour performed the song live for his entrance at WrestleMania 29" (passage 1); Bridge / missing fact: the venue/stadium used for WrestleMania 29 (the New Jersey stadium) must be identified; Retrieval plan: (1) open the "WrestleMania 29" page (e.g., WrestleMania 29 Wikipedia or WWE event page) and inspect the Infobox/Venue or Location section for the stadium name, or (2) search "WrestleMania 29 venue" site:wikipedia.org; Disambiguation: If you mean a different CM Punk entrance/event, please specify which event.
hop2_docs: WrestleMania 29 | WrestleMania 29 (billed in print ads as WrestleMania NY/NJ) was the twenty-ninth annual WrestleMania professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) event produced by WWE, held on April 7, 2013 at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey.  Nine professional wrestling matches were contested at the event, with one being shown on the pre-show.

WrestleMania XXVI | WrestleMania XXVI was the twenty-sixth annual WrestleMania professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) event produced by World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE), which took place on March 28, 2010, at the University of Phoenix Stadium in the Phoenix suburb of Glendale, Arizona.  This was the first WrestleMania since WrestleMania XI with a non-title match as a main event, the first WrestleMania to be held in the state of Arizona and the third to be held in an open-air venue, after WrestleMania IX and WrestleMania XXIV.

WrestleMania 31 | WrestleMania 31 was the thirty-first annual WrestleMania professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) event produced by WWE.  It took place on March 29, 2015, at Levi's Stadium in Santa Clara, California.

Giants Stadium | Giants Stadium (Sometimes referred to as Giants Stadium at the Meadowlands or The Swamp), was a stadium located in East Rutherford, New Jersey, in the Meadowlands Sports Complex.  The venue had been open between 1976 and 2010, and it primarily hosted sporting events and concerts in its history.  The maximum seating capacity was 80,242.  The structure itself was 756 ft long, 592 ft wide and 144 ft high from service level to the top of the seating bowl and 178 ft high to the top of the south tower.  The volume of the stadium was 64500000 cuft .  13,500 tons of structural steel were used in the building process and 29,200 tons of concrete were poured.  It was owned and operated by the New Jersey Sports and Exposition Authority (NJSEA).

WrestleMania 2 | WrestleMania 2 (sequentially known as WrestleMania II) was the second annual WrestleMania professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) event produced by the World Wrestling Federation (WWF) (although the first WrestleMania was only on pay-per-view in select areas).  It took place on Monday, April 7, 1986, making it the only WrestleMania that was not held on the customary Sunday.  WrestleMania 2 took place at three venues: the Nassau Veterans Memorial Coliseum in Uniondale, New York; the Rosemont Horizon in Rosemont, Illinois; and the Los Angeles Memorial Sports Arena in Los Angeles, California.

WrestleMania IV | WrestleMania IV was the fourth annual WrestleMania sports entertainment pay-per-view (PPV) event produced by the World Wrestling Federation (WWF).  It took place on March 27, 1988, at the Historic Atlantic City Convention Hall in Atlantic City, New Jersey.  The announced attendance of WrestleMania IV was 19,199.

WrestleMania V | WrestleMania V was the fifth annual WrestleMania professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) and closed-circuit TV event produced by the World Wrestling Federation (WWF).  It took place on April 2, 1989, at the Boardwalk Hall in Atlantic City, New Jersey.  The event was commentated by Gorilla Monsoon and Jesse Ventura.
response.summary_2: Claim: Living Colour performed "Cult of Personality" live for CM Punk's entrance at WrestleMania 29. WrestleMania 29 (passage [1]) states the event “was ... held ... at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey” (explicit support: MetLife Stadium). Passages [2],[3],[5],[6],[7] provide metadata on other WrestleMania venues (University of Phoenix Stadium, Levi's Stadium, Nassau Veterans Memorial Coliseum, Historic Atlantic City Convention Hall, Boardwalk Hall) and passage [4] supplies Giants Stadium history in East Rutherford. Integration: the retrieved evidence explicitly identifies MetLife Stadium (passage [1]) as the New Jersey stadium for WrestleMania 29; the other passages only supply related venue metadata.
